sections.content:

  • Write your article
  • Use headings from h2 to h5
  • Use heading 6 to caption images
  • Make images full width and set to “break text”
  • Inserting images
  • Suggest using “Search the web”:

sections.title: Article Title
sections.content: XXX reports

sections.title: Chairman's Chat
sections.content:
Tam (AKA Glorious Leader) reports

What a great  experience on Windbank on 30th May (written up by Pete Logan further on this newsletter). That what’s being in a supportive club is all about. A coaching / 10-100 club day evolving out of collective decisions to get the best out of the day. Ok so we didn’t all get great flights, but plenty got plenty of information from our own experts on how they go about route selection on the day, the best route to take when getting away from the hill, height required, what timings to aim for etc. Even details of airspace downwind from an ex RAF fast jet pilot - come on where else can you get this sort of coaching! Many thanks to Pete, Jake and Simon (and later, Ed) for the input, it was great to be part of it. Let’s have more of these days. Unfortunately for me, I didn’t get to put it all into practice, though I did bomb out further up the valley than I ever have done before, and was rewarded with a walk virtually all the way back from Litton. During the briefing we all witnessed someone taking off with a cravat, I am not sure who. The short flight looked pretty scary but fortunately the landing was good enough. Luckily as we were all gathered anyway, being briefed, Jake was able to give an immediate explanation of the inputs that should have been made to make the incident a virtual non event. I’m sure many learned a very quick and valuable lesson!

The club is also offering practical (cash!) help if you are interested in becoming a BHPA qualified repacker. There are costs associated with this. Recent years have seen it become more difficult to get a repack organised. The committee wondered if any member would be interested in becoming a qualified repacker, who could then run the club repack as well as have a useful side income. A useful earner - if I were a bit younger I would certainly be interested!  The club is willing to pay half the course fee (so around £200) if anyone wishes to consider this. It takes quite a bit more than just 1 course to become qualified, but this is the early costly bit.

The Snaizeholme (Dodd Fell) TDA will become active this month. Arrangements are currently being finalised and will be on the site guide - see Simon’s article further on in this edition. Please ensure you are up to date on requirements and responsibilities. We all owe Simon and Rob a couple of beers for their efforts on this.

Fly safe everyone. We can’t all be skygods, but that shouldn’t stop us cherishing every moment in our amazing sport. Well, most moments anyway!

sections.title: Coaching
sections.content: Pete Logan, Chief Coach reports

sections.title: Sites and News
sections.content:
Pete Logan reports

We’re keeping an eye out for the right conditions for the Baildon Sod from now on. Ideally a very light easterly on a midweek evening. The competition is open to all members and consists of trying to achieve the longest glide of the night whilst avoiding bracken, ticks, Lime’s disease and lapses in dignity. We’re also considering a H&F award for the person that does the most repetitions of top to bottoms.

Refreshments, chips and fish in the pub afterwards. Keep your eye out for news of it on the social channels.

sections.title: What’s in a name: Hawkswick, Windbank or Knipe Scar?
sections.content:
Martin Baxter speculates

One of our more popular sites is the SW facing ridge overlooking Littondale.  It’s a long, novice friendly site with good XC potential to the NE coast and, being in the southern Dales, is a shorter drive for most of our members. In the early days of the club it was the place where hang glider pilots would leave a note in the wall if they were driving further North.

DHPC Site Guide - Windbank/Hawkswick

There is a take-off above the village of Hawkswick which I think we all agree is called, erm, Hawkswick.  It doesn’t get used much.  It’s a bit higher and steeper than the other option, so a bigger walk up; parking and local residents can be problematic (there have been reports of nail traps in the past); and since the farmer asked us not to use the massive field by the bend in the river, we’ve been limited to a much smaller bottom landing field which reportedly suffers from rotor in strong winds.  Why bother when there is a much better site just down the ridge with easier access?

OK, cards on the table: I’ve always known this more popular site as Windbank, but some people refer to it as Knipe Scar.  Easy parking and bottom landing (as long as you don’t overshoot into the river or wires!), relatively easy walk up and plenty of room for taking off, slope and top landing.  Following a change of ownership we also appear to be getting away without paying for parking (shhhh!).

Ordnance Survey 1:50,000 Scale Mapping

The lower slopes, marked on the map as High Wind Bank, used to be used for training.  I suspect that when students progressed to the upper ridge they referred to it as Knipe Scar, eager to make the distinction that they were now Sky Gods, but to me it’s still Windbank.  The Ordnance Survey 1:50K scale map isn’t particularly conclusive since Knipe Scar is depicted bang on top of the spur which faces both South West and East.  However if you zoom in to the 1:25K scale map it’s clear (to me anyway) that Knipe Scar is the East facing outcrop.  There is another clue: further North, also on the East facing slope, are Knipe Close, Knipe Barn, and Knipe Wood.  And surely the car park is best described as Windbank?  I rest my case M’lord.

Ordnance Survey 1:25,000 Scale Mapping

Does any of this matter?  Well, if you’re familiar with the site, then probably not.  But spare a thought for novices.  A few years ago some experienced pilots posted that they were planning to fly at Knipe Scar.  Two novices Googled Knipe Scar and were all set to drive to the Lake District!  Try it - you’ll see what I mean.  It’s helpful if we all use the same terminology.

One final thought.  When we negotiated the site on the East facing slope, we considered calling it Hawkswick East or Knipe Scar, which would have confused things even more.  In the end the Spilletts came up with the very sensible suggestion of calling it Kettlewell.  Let common sense prevail!

 DHPC Site Guide - Kettlewell

sections.title: Northern Challenge Series (Hike & Fly)
sections.content:
Ed Cleasby/Adam Westhead

We have made some radical changes to the Hike & Fly series.  By the time you read this they should be live on xcmap, with the direct link below. Additional information can be found by going to my website (link below) where a number of supporting  pdf files may be useful.

https://www.xcmap.net/index.php?c=NCT%20Hike%20and%20Fly&y=2026

https://www.xcflight.com/northern-challenge-series-2026/

The changes and the reasons are explained  below.

The H&F series now totals eleven - five are Lakes based and six Dales based . The Dales Three Peaks has been retained as it’s iconic, has a history of attempts although longer by far than any of the other tasks. The ten new tasks are designed to be less arduous, allow more flying and provide  interesting fun challenges where ordinary weight kit is less  a factor.. Almost all are valley based and set around well known walking circuits, rounds and horseshoes.  In the right conditions they have large flyable sections and can accommodate different wind directions …and now cover  winter attempts..  The aim is to provide a wider range of more accessible, interesting and inclusive tasks across the full year.

The scoring algorithm remains the same for all tasks regardless of length, height gain, number of turnpoints or conditions. They are all of a fairly similar level, but with odd exceptions. The whole series is now open all year round allowing winter attempts and rewarding  these with a new award.The qualifying dates are set out  below

Whole year award. This runs from 31st October -  31st October the following year. The highest total on the scoreboard  on that date wins.

Winter award.   This runs from 31st Oct - 31st March.  The highest winter total on the scoreboard on the closing  date wins. These winter scores also carry through into the full year scores. We will use scoreboard wiping after 1st October to identify winter flights although they are igc dated.

The intention is to relax the challenge level a little and attract more pilots to consider hike and fly as not just for the super fit, the masochistic  or the off days. They are set such that attempting in winter is accessible, a nice level of challenge, a way of staying fit and  active whilst  providing lots of fly down opportunities. A few have also had TP’s tweaked and sized to form small FAI triangles that on a good flying day could be  flown in their entirety.

I’m grateful to Adam Westhead for providing both assistance in designing the Dales tasks and providing the initial jolt, along with Chris Foster who manages the whole thing on xcmap.

MAIN SERIES (update at 28/5/2026). Current leader is Adam Westhead. Adam has attempted four tasks and made goal on three.

https://www.xcmap.net/

sections.title: 10-100 XC Coaching Day - Saturday 30th
sections.content:
Pete Logan reports

The briefing was on Hawkswick (did I get that right Martin??) and the task was for a downwind run to the coast at Seaham just south of Sunderland. Maybe twenty folks there, all keen on trying to make the best of the day. A blue sky with a lot of milky high cloud in places seemed to be stopping Cu forming on the hill itself but we had fairly ideal general flying conditions and on reflection it might have turned out to be a better day for doing Ed’s NCS Windbank task on the hill itself. It was certainly not an ideal day for going over the back….

You can see the less than inspiring state of the sky.

After several false starts and a few brave individuals had thrown themselves over onto Buckden Pike, the gaggle decided that after nearly two hours on the hill and several false starts we should actually dare to go over the back. The initial delay had been that we were waiting to see the development of Cu over the back to signal that it was working and that just didn’t happen on the day. Eight of us dribbled onto Starbotton and then into the bowl of Cam Head. We found our climb out and committed to the high ground between Coverdale and Bishopdale, making for Penhill.

Jake called the valley crossing of Wharfedale and we headed directly north with the intention to go west of Bellerby and Feldom ranges after topping up from a distant Fremington Edge. Things did not go to plan. 😬 We crossed onto Redmire Moor which you could normally expect to work and mainly it didn’t so now all eight of us are low on the descent into Swaledale, east of Reeth and desperate for a climb out. This is where gaggles necessarily start to split. When you’re low you are committed to your own search area and you can be cut off from someone else who looks like they found a decent climb just a few hundred metres away.

You hang onto what you find and Geoff and I managed to establish a climb out of the tops above Fremington Edge heading straight for the middle of Feldom which was not optimal. We certainly didn’t have the height to clear it without taking a big risk. We made for the west side which is handily marked by a V shaped copse and car park at Long Green Gate. That joined us up with Neil and John and again, not high enough, we slid off the high moors into the A66 valley just heading for ground with weak sun. Apparently it’s called the Greta Valley, who knew?

And Then There Were Three… Needing a climb, we tend to stick to our own bits of the sky that are working right now for us and we found ourselves separated from Neil. Barnard Castle passed by and we approached Bishop Aukland at about mid height. Even when it looks like you’re out on the flats you’re often not and there was a useful looking hill with a couple of antenna towers just south of the town. We head over and get an extra 1000ft which was worth it as a sustainer.

Route choice now is now determined by the weak sun on the ground. There’s a huge blanket of grey making its way over from the west, gradually shutting off the sky to our left and behind. We’re hopeful of a climb at Spennymoor, especially John and I as we had a poor line and our glides have taken us lower than Geoff. John goes for the town centre but gets more sink and I am hopeful of an industrial estate at the east edge of town. Neither work 😬. At this point you’re picking landable fields and I usually keep two in mind that can be reached. Helpfully, one is on the front lawn of Croxdale Hall which might work out nicely if I land during Afternoon Tea. Some local Pease Pudding on a Stottie Cake would go down quite nicely.

At this point, my thoughts are more about a safe landing and maximising my glide towards Durham and an easy train retrieve. Separately, Geoff was thinking that if I landed he would too as the sky was closing in. Luck had other ideas. I feel a twitch and make a few circles, descending at first, but there’s something to hook into here. I get down to 230m AGL and then find it, thankfully. It goes all the way to the top at 1600m - that’s fifty 360s and 20 minutes, 24 seconds per 360 so it was a bit of a wider thermal, average 1.1m/s - nothing out of the ordinary for the day.

Seaham docks with Peterlee & Teeside in the distance:

Looking up to Ryhope, Washington & Sunderland with Tynemouth further on:

Geoff demonstrates an uninspiring sky:

https://www.xcontest.org/world/en/flights/detail:vikingforties/30.05.2026/10:28

sections.title: Safety: Check your zips, both ‘in’ and ‘out’ !!
sections.content:
Rob Urselmann reports

If you haven’t already seen the learnings from the CSC repack day, this is a must read !  
Copied here with both permission and express support from Jocky.

During the last zip event there were 3 deployments that failed. One of which was during the initial deployment - so, an actual reserve in situ that would not have deployed.

This from Pat…

All 3 were to do with the zip closures and one of the zippers being left at the handle end of the zip, this blocks the opening of the container. The 2 on dummies could be excused as pilots were rushed or stressed when they were repacking the dummies (these were on the 2nd or 3rd attempts and the repacking had been done by themselves).

The important one. A pilot had his reserve installed and the zipper had been left in the wrong place. He’d been flying with it like that and wouldn’t have known except that he couldn’t extract it during the initial test before putting the dummy reserve in. Please always check that your zipper goes to the end of the zip and then is returned to the housing in the harness, away from the opening end.

This is an example of one with a zip blocking the deployment. This could not be deployed !!

There were a couple of gentle attempts that got stuck during deployment that came out with a second, more confident tug.

There was one who struggled to extract his reserve because he was pulling the handle up towards him, this Jams/twists the reserve against the underside of the harness and creates a lot of friction making it harder to deploy. Most handles need to be pulled away from you.

This from Richard…

From my point of view, pilots need to be more assertive and committed with their throws. There were some very weak and half hearted throws, in a real situation this may have been different but it needs to be practiced.

I also think it’s really bad that pilots have no idea what they’re reserve is half the time and have no idea how to install it in their harness. If they had an accidental deployment on the hill they would struggle to fit it back in themselves.

To conclude.

• Make sure you check the zipper is back in its housing away from the opening end.

• Be positive and confident with your throw

• Pull and throw the reserve out away from you and not up towards you.

• Learn and practice the final stages of packing your reserve into your harness.

• Look, locate, grasp, pull & throw

If you have any questions about safety please contact me or the coaching team. Thanks

Jocky

Further to this, there is also the other event: The zips coming undone from the reserve opening (my Delight3 was notorious for this)

Please make checking your reserve zips an integral part of your pre-flight checks.

sections.title: TDA at Dodd Fell
sections.content:
Simon Hulme Reports…
All DHPC members who have read recent issues of Skywords will be familiar with the Airspace Change Proposal submitted by Menapia for a study of cloud formation using a BVLOS drone in Snaizeholme Fell just below the Dodd Fell take off.  Copied verbatim below is the article I submitted for Skywings to bring broader awareness to the issue for all those BHPA members who may use Dodd Fell.  Since I submitted that article the CAA have approved the airspace and the trial will go ahead from 25 June - 30 November 2026.  However, things are not as bad as some may have feared - there are large periods when activity will not be taking place and the Woodland Trust, who own the land that Menapia will fly from, have asked Menapia to limit activity to 2 days a week.  We are still waiting for the last few bits of information from Menapia before we update the site guide; we will let everyone know when that’s been done.
We have agreed on a no fly window as stated in the article below and for planning purposes we have agreed UKV on WINDY as the forecasting model on which planning decisions will be made.  When Menapia launches the drone from the valley it has to be turned into wind as part of its operating profile; if the wind turns out to be in the agreed no fly window they will have to terminate the flight.  
The telephone hotline for TDA status is 0113 5268554 and this should be checked before deciding to go to launch - it will be updated whenever the operating status changes. We are still waiting for the website link and the sign to go up at the parking area on the Pennine Way.  
PS they can still do meaningful data gathering when there is no cloud!  
Approval imminent for Drone TDA at Snaizeholme aka Dodd Fell, near Hawes in the Yorkshire Dales
 
Location: 1km radius circle centred at the woodland trust site at 54° 15′ 57.89′′ N, 2° 15′ 48.10′′ W (54.26608, -2.26336) — OS Grid: SD 82944 85634 Surface to 7000 feet AMSL
 
Active by NOTAM Monday to Friday 0900-1700L alternate window 0500-1000L
 
The DHPC have been actively engaged over the last few months with Leeds based drone company Menapia, who have submitted an airspace change proposal under CAP 1616G to support the operation of a beyond visual line of sight drone at Snaizeholme Fell, approximately 6km southwest of Hawes.  The drone, which is fitted with unique sensors, will be flying a tightly controlled vertical profile at the centre of the TDA, multiple times a day, to conduct cloud particle sampling in support of a scientific study being run by the University of Leeds.  Cloud formation is a key driver of weather patterns, and this trial will ultimately help to shape and refine the weather forecasting models we use every day as free flight enthusiasts.  
 
The proposed TDA is adjacent to Dodd Fell take off, which is a DHPC published site open to all BHPA pilots.  When the TDA is active it effectively prevents the safe use of the site, so it was essential that we worked collaboratively with Menapia to achieve a solution that was agreeable to both parties.  
 
Menapia have agreed that when the forecast wind is within the window 260-325 degrees (Dodd Fell wind rose +/- 10 degrees) they will cease flying activity by 1000L.  They will maintain a telephone hotline that will be updated daily with a recorded message giving the status of the TDA and there will be new signage at the take-off parking area.   The DHPC site guide will be updated when all the data we need is published by the CAA.
 
Menapia will submit NOTAMs not less than 24 hours ahead of planned operations but given the vagaries of the weather this may lead to the situation where we have an active NOTAM but due to the no-fly agreement, stated above, Menapia will not be flying.  It is therefore essential that any pilot planning to fly from Dodd Fell during the trial period reads the updated DHPC site guide and phones the hotline telephone number listed on the website before driving to the take-off site – NB there is no telephone signal at launch and Menapia will not be operating any airspace crossing service. If in doubt about the status of the TDA, please do not fly from Dodd Fell.    
 
As acknowledged by Menapia in their final ACP submission a TDA is not prohibited airspace, and pilots are not legally obliged under SERA to avoid it; however, there is dangerous activity taking place and pilots who infringe the TDA will be at increased risk of loss of safe separation.  Menapia will also be obliged both to conduct an emergency landing with the drone and to submit an occurrence report to the CAA if they observe other aircraft that infringe or are likely to infringe the TDA.  
 
We have lost some flexibility, and this may frustrate some pilots, but the DHPC Committee request that you all respect the agreements we have reached with Menapia. This is a short-term trial, and the long-term plan is to use an existing danger area such as Warcop, that will have much less impact on our community.  We accept that we may lose the odd day when the weather at launch is different to forecast. Some pilots may have to delay their takeoff, but Dodd Fell is very rarely flown before 1100L.  We also acknowledge that there will also be a smaller but still significant impact for pilots flying XC from other sites.  
 
For more info go to the CAA Airspace Change Portal, search ACP-2025-053 and read the Final Proposal Document – its only 114 pages.
